title: Axobotl
subtitle: A low-cost social robot that helps students and office workers build healthier habits
teaser: A friendly, affordable desktop robot — inspired by the Mexican axolotl — that encourages healthy habits among students and office workers.
intro: Chronic diseases like diabetes, hypertension, and depression are rising worldwide — and the best medicine is prevention: better sleep, more movement, less stress. Axobotl is a small, friendly desktop robot designed to sit where those habits are formed (and broken): on the desks of students and office workers, gently encouraging healthier routines.

why.text: Most social robots are too expensive for the schools, clinics, and communities that could benefit most — especially in developing countries. Axobotl is built from widely available parts: a smartphone as its face, a Raspberry Pi as its brain, 3D-printed body, and affordable servo motors. Care technology should not be a luxury.

sections.title: Designed with its future users, not just for them
sections.text: Axobotl was created through design thinking and participatory design: students and staff took part in the ideation, definition, and prototyping phases, shaping what the robot should do and how it should feel. Its identity is inspired by the axolotl — a beloved Mexican symbol of regeneration — making it approachable rather than intimidating.

sections.title: Modern AI in an affordable body
sections.text: Despite its low cost, Axobotl is no toy: its smartphone face gives it a touch display, camera, and microphone, while its Raspberry Pi controller and servo-actuated movements enable expressive, interactive behaviors — and the integration of state-of-the-art AI for conversation and perception. It is a first step toward democratizing social robotics for preventive healthcare.

sections.title: A collaboration across continents
sections.text: Axobotl is developed between AIST in Japan and the Benemérita Universidad Autónoma de Puebla in Mexico — an example of how international collaboration can aim technology directly at well-being and health equity, rather than only at industrial performance.
